design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate frog from bottom to top of screen by dodging moving obstacles (cars, river hazards) within time limit; repeat across five frogs per level
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time pressure combined with spatial hazard avoidance; multiple simultaneous moving threats with no safe zones
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Master reflexive navigation through deadly environments; solve dynamic spatial puzzles under pressure
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Frogger is deliberately unforgiving in its design philosophy—1982 coverage noted it as 'the arcade game with the most ways to die.' Every screen element is a threat; there are no safe spaces except destination homes. The game enforces extreme precision: wrong timing, wrong direction, or positioning off-screen all result in instant death. This is a statement about arcade difficulty being the primary game mechanic, not a secondary challenge layer.

difficulty.lives_system: Player starts with three, five, or seven frogs depending on arcade machine settings; losing all frogs triggers game over
difficulty.checkpointing: Each frog reaching home safely resets timer to 60 ticks and progresses toward level completion; no mid-level checkpoints within a single frog's attempt
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited to machine configuration at start (frog count selection). Difficulty curve is fixed: progressive increase for five levels, brief ease, then renewed increase. No selectable difficulty modes documented.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Level progression tied to successfully routing all five frogs home. Increased difficulty (faster moving obstacles, tighter timing) unlocks as reward/punishment after each level
replayability_hooks: High score tracking with 20,000-point milestone awarding bonus frog
Multiple ways to die encouraging skill mastery for optimization
Alternating two-player mode extending session length
Difficulty curve cycling (five hard levels, one ease, repeat harder)

world_building_techniques: Thematic environmental design: urban road hazards contrasted with natural river navigation
Visual iconography: skull-and-crossbones death markers, female frog bonus character, fly pickup (200 points)
Audio theming: opening uses Japanese children's song 'Inu no Omawarisan' plus anime references, creating playful contrast with deadly mechanics

danger_signals: Hazard lanes with opposing traffic patterns demand constant directional awareness
River section combines three simultaneous threats: logs, turtles, alligators—all moving at different speeds
Time pressure (30-second per-frog countdown, reset to 60 ticks per level start) creates stress manifestation
environmental_storytelling: Road section represents urban human infrastructure as lethal force
River section represents natural predators and environmental instability
Frog homes as safe haven/goal structure provides clear spatial narrative
